title: Fermi energy Weyl nodes in $\mathbf{AM}$Te$_4$ ($\mathbf{A}$=Ta, Nb, $\mathbf{M}$=Ir, Rh)
authors: Shivam Parasar, Jeroen van den Brink, Rajyavardhan Ray
abstract: Key aspects of the quantum oscillations and magnetoresistance in Weyl semimetals $AM$Te$_4$ ($A$=Nb,Ta, $M$=Rh, Ir) persist as open questions, obscuring the link between their topological electronic structure and practical implementations. Employing a generalised search procedure, we carry out a comprehensive scan of WPs accounting for all the subbands close to the Fermi energy, and show that this dramatically alters the WP landscape in these compounds. In particular, we predict these compounds to feature WPs within a few meV of the Fermi energy which significantly influence their properties. Remarkably, most of the considered compounds host WPs of more than one type, including NbRhTe$_4$ which hosts type-I, II and III Weyl points. Our comparative analysis of structure and fidelity of computational parameters/models not only provides a detailed mapping of the complex electronic structure in these compounds, but also clarifies quantum oscillations and magnetoresistance observations in this family, bridging the gap between theory and experiments and offering a framework for precise tunability of WPs.
summary: This paper provides a more accurate mapping of the Weyl node landscape in AMTe4 semimetals by considering multiple electronic subbands. It successfully bridges the gap between theory and experiment by showing that Weyl nodes located very close to the Fermi energy are responsible for previously unexplained transport phenomena.
main_problem: The study addresses discrepancies between theoretical predictions and experimental observations (such as quantum oscillations and magnetoresistance) in AMTe4 Weyl semimetals, specifically regarding the true topological landscape of Weyl nodes.
main_result: The authors reveal a 'tiered' Weyl node landscape where many nodes exist much closer to the Fermi energy than previously thought, including the discovery of Type-III Weyl nodes and a unified explanation for anomalous transport features like Hall kinks and non-saturating magnetoresistance.
method: The researchers employed Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the FPLO code with LDA and GGA functionals, combined with Wannier models and a generalized search procedure that accounts for multiple subbands near the Fermi energy.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of a family of layered, noncentrosymmetric orthorhombic transition metal tellurides, specifically AMTe4 where A = Ta, Nb and M = Ir, Rh.
key_observables: Weyl point energy and type (I, II, III), chirality, Fermi surface topology (electron/hole pockets), magnetoresistance scaling (B^1.5 vs B^2), de Haas-van Alphen frequencies, and Hall effect kinks.
important_parameters: Fermi energy (Ef), Weyl point energy (Ewp), magnetic field (B), Berry curvature, and chemical pressure (substitution of Ir with Rh).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of DFT-based approximations (LDA/GGA) and relies on the accuracy of structural relaxation models to represent experimental reality.
figures_summary: Figures include Weyl node positions and energy distributions in the Brillouin zone, Fermi surface topologies (including hole and electron pockets), and the angular dependence of experimental dHvA frequencies.
paper_structure: The paper begins by identifying the discrepancy between theory and experiment, presents a new computational approach for scanning subbands, details the resulting Weyl node landscape and types, compares different material compositions (Ta vs Nb), and concludes by linking these topological features to observed magnetotransport anomalies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Crystal structure and electronic properties. (a) Crystal structure of AMTe4 (A=Ta, Nb, M=Ir, Rh). (b-c) Electronic band structure for (b) the experimental crystal structure of TaIrTe4, and (c) the optimzed crystal structure of TaRhTe4, using LDA and 20×10×10 k-mesh. (d) Band structures of TaIrTe4 for the optimized crystal structure with GGA and 20 × 10 × 10 k-mesh (solid line), and the experiemntal crystal structure with LDA and 12 × 6 × 6 k-mesh (dashed line). (e) Comparison between band structure of TaRhTe4 using LDA (solid line) and GGA (dashed line) exchange correlation functional for 20 × 10 × 10 k-mesh. (f) Calculated Fermi surface of TaIrTe4 within GGA. (g) Calculated Fermi...

Figure 2: WP landscape for Ta systems. (a-b) Energy distribution of WPs for (a) TaIrTe4 and (b) TaRhTe4, accounting for different crystal structures and exchange-correlation functional. WP quartets are represented by dark thin bars while octets are represented by light-colored thick bars. (c-d) The in-plane coordinates (kx, ky) of the WPs in (c) TaIrTe4, and (d) TaRhTe4, obtained using GGA.

Figure 3: Angular dependance of dHvA frequencies in Ta-based systems. (a) The observed dHvA frequencies from Ref. [24]. (b-c) Calculated angular dependance of dHvA for (b) optimized structure of TaIrTe4, and (c) otpimized structure of TaRhTe4. In all cases, k-mesh with 20 × 10 × 10 intervals were used together with GGA.

Figure 4: Electronic structure and topology of Nb compounds. (a-b) Electronic band structure for optimized crystal structure of (a) NbIrTe4 and (b) NbRhTe4. (c-d) WP landscape in (c) NbIrTe4 and (d) NbRhTe4. Other details same as in Fig. 2.
Figure 5: Angular dependence of dHvA in Nb compounds. (a-b) Calculated angular dependence of dHvA for optimized crystal structure of (a) NbIrTe4 and (b) NbRhTe4
